title: stwd 3.2.0
text:
stwd 0.0.0.9000

Initial development version
Added theme_ibcs() for IBCS-compliant ggplot2 theme
Added ibcs_colors() for standardized color palette
Added scenario notation scales: scale_fill_ibcs_scenario(), scale_color_ibcs_scenario()
Added variance visualization: ibcs_variance_chart(), scale_fill_ibcs_variance()
Added ibcs_waterfall() for waterfall charts
Added ibcs_bar_chart() for bar charts with scenario notation
Added multi-panel layout functions: one_to_split_to_two_layout(), two_to_merge_to_one_layout()
Added make_connector() for standalone connector elements
Added add_panel_labels() for patchwork plot labeling
Added marquee integration: marquee_color_labels(), geom_marquee_colored()

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text: Getting consistent sizing between story_designer() and Quarto documents requires matching the figure dimensions. This guide explains how to ensure your story layouts render identically in both environments.

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text: Getting consistent sizing between story_designer() and Quarto documents requires matching the figure dimensions. This guide explains how to ensure your story layouts render identically in both environments.

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
The Sizing Challenge
Text sizes in ggplot2 are absolute (points/mm), but plot dimensions are relative. When Quarto renders at different dimensions than you designed for, the text-to-plot ratio shifts, causing:

Titles that appear too large or too small
Clipped text that fit perfectly in the designer
Narrative panels with unexpected proportions

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
Matching Story Designer to Quarto

Step 1: Design in Story Designer
Launch the designer and adjust your layout:

library(stwd)
library(ggplot2)

p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(factor(cyl), mpg)) + geom_boxplot()
story_designer(plot = p)



Step 2: Note Your Export Dimensions
In the Validate & Export tab, note the settings:

Width (in): e.g., 12
Height (in): e.g., 9
DPI: e.g., 150

Click Validate Actual Size to preview exactly what your export will look like.


Step 3: Copy the Generated Code
Go to the Code tab and copy the generated patchwork code.


Step 4: Match Quarto Chunk Options
Use the same dimensions in your Quarto code chunk:

# Paste the generated code from story_designer()
title_plot <- title_block("**Your Title**", title_size = 12)
subtitle_plot <- subtitle_block("Subtitle", subtitle_size = 10)
narrative_plot <- text_narrative("Your narrative...", size = 10)
caption_plot <- caption_block("Source: Data", caption_size = 8)

content <- p + narrative_plot + plot_layout(widths = c(0.65, 0.35))
title_plot / subtitle_plot / content / caption_plot +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.82, 0.05))

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
Quarto Chunk Options Reference



Option
Description
Example




fig-width
Plot width in inches
12


fig-height
Plot height in inches
9


fig-dpi
Resolution (dots per inch)
150


out-width
Display width in document
"100%"


fig-format
Output format
"png", "svg"




Document-Level Defaults
Set defaults in your YAML header to avoid repeating options:
---
title: "My Report"
format:
  html:
    fig-width: 12
    fig-height: 9
    fig-dpi: 150
---
Then individual chunks inherit these defaults.

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
Alternative: Export as PNG
For maximum control and consistency, export from R and embed the image:

# Build the layout with block functions
title_plot <- title_block("**Q4 Sales Report**", title_size = 12)
content <- p + text_narrative("Key findings...", size = 10) +
    plot_layout(widths = c(0.65, 0.35))
layout <- title_plot / content + plot_layout(heights = c(0.1, 0.9))

# Save with exact dimensions
ggsave("figures/sales_report.png", layout,
       width = 12, height = 9, dpi = 150, bg = "white")

Then embed in your Quarto document:
Advantages:

Guaranteed consistency with Story Designer preview
Faster document rendering (no R code execution)
Easy to version control the output

Disadvantages:

Manual regeneration when data changes
Separate files to manage

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
Common Issues

Text appears larger/smaller than expected
Cause: fig-width or fig-height doesn’t match your design dimensions.
Fix: Match the exact dimensions from Story Designer’s export tab.


Title or narrative is clipped
Cause: The height proportions were designed for different dimensions.
Fix: Adjust the plot_layout(heights = ...) values:

# Increase title height proportion
title_plot / subtitle_plot / content / caption_plot +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.12, 0.06, 0.77, 0.05))



Plot looks blurry
Cause: Low DPI setting.
Fix: Increase fig-dpi to 150 or 300:

# Your patchwork code...



Inconsistent sizing across chunks
Cause: Different chunks have different fig-width/fig-height.
Fix: Set document-level defaults in YAML, or use consistent chunk options.

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
Recommended Dimensions



Use Case
Width
Height
Aspect




Presentation (16:9)
12
6.75
1.78


Presentation (4:3)
10
7.5
1.33


Report (full width)
10
7
1.43


Report (half width)
6
4.5
1.33


Square
8
8
1.00

title: Sizing Story Layouts for Quarto
text:
Summary

Design your layout in story_designer()
Use Validate Actual Size to confirm appearance
Copy the Width, Height, and DPI values
Copy the generated code from the Code tab
Set matching fig-width, fig-height, fig-dpi in Quarto chunks
Add out-width: "100%" for responsive display

For critical presentations, export as PNG for guaranteed consistency.

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
The marquee package provides rich text formatting for R graphics, including the ability to color text inline. The stwd package integrates with marquee to create labels where the text color automatically matches data values. ## Why Colored Labels?
In data visualizations, you often want labels to match their meaning:

Green text for positive variances
Red text for negative variances

This creates visual consistency and immediate comprehension.

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
The marquee package provides rich text formatting for R graphics, including the ability to color text inline. The stwd package integrates with marquee to create labels where the text color automatically matches data values. ## Why Colored Labels?
In data visualizations, you often want labels to match their meaning:

Green text for positive variances
Red text for negative variances

This creates visual consistency and immediate comprehension.

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
The marquee_color_labels() Function
This function formats text labels with marquee color syntax:

# Basic usage
labels <- c("Good", "Bad", "Neutral")
colors <- c("#006400", "#8B0000", "#404040")

marquee_color_labels(labels, colors)

[1] "{#006400 Good}"    "{#8B0000 Bad}"     "{#404040 Neutral}"



With Formatting Options

# Bold labels
marquee_color_labels(labels, colors, bold = TRUE)

[1] "{#006400 **Good**}"    "{#8B0000 **Bad**}"     "{#404040 **Neutral**}"



# Italic labels
marquee_color_labels(labels, colors, italic = TRUE)

[1] "{#006400 *Good*}"    "{#8B0000 *Bad*}"     "{#404040 *Neutral*}"



# Bold and italic
marquee_color_labels(labels, colors, bold = TRUE, italic = TRUE)

[1] "{#006400 ***Good***}"    "{#8B0000 ***Bad***}"    
[3] "{#404040 ***Neutral***}"

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
Using with ggplot2 and marquee

Manual Approach
You can use marquee_color_labels() to pre-format your data, then use marquee::geom_marquee():

library(marquee)

#  Define colors for examples
cols <- list(actual = "#404040", variance_positive = "#006400", variance_negative = "#8B0000", previous = "#A0A0A0")

# Prepare data
variance_data <- data.frame(
    category = c("Product A", "Product B", "Product C", "Product D"),
    value = c(120, 85, 105, 95),
    variance = c(20, -5, 5, -5)
)

# Determine colors based on variance
variance_data$text_color <- ifelse(
    variance_data$variance >= 0,
    cols$variance_positive,
    cols$variance_negative
)

# Create formatted labels
variance_data$formatted_label <- marquee_color_labels(
    sprintf("%+d", variance_data$variance),
    variance_data$text_color,
    bold = TRUE
)

# Plot with marquee
ggplot(variance_data, aes(x = category, y = value)) +
    geom_col(fill = cols$actual, width = 0.6) +
    geom_marquee(
        aes(label = formatted_label),
        vjust = -0.5,
        size = 4
    ) +
    labs(title = "Sales with Colored Variance Labels") +
    theme_minimal()



Using geom_marquee_colored()
For convenience, use geom_marquee_colored() which handles the formatting automatically:

library(marquee)

variance_data$label <- sprintf("%+d", variance_data$variance)

ggplot(variance_data, aes(x = category, y = value)) +
    geom_col(fill = cols$actual, width = 0.6) +
    geom_marquee_colored(
        data = variance_data,
        mapping = aes(x = category, y = value + 5, label = label, color = text_color),
        bold = TRUE,
        size = 4
    ) +
    labs(title = "Sales with Auto-Colored Variance Labels") +
    theme_minimal()

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
Practical Examples

Variance Chart with Colored Labels

library(marquee)

#  Define colors for examples
cols <- list(actual = "#404040", variance_positive = "#006400", variance_negative = "#8B0000", previous = "#A0A0A0")

# Sales performance data
sales <- data.frame(
    region = c("North", "South", "East", "West", "Central"),
    actual = c(150, 120, 180, 95, 110),
    plan = c(140, 130, 170, 100, 100)
)

sales$variance <- sales$actual - sales$plan
sales$variance_pct <- round((sales$variance / sales$plan) * 100, 1)
sales$var_color <- ifelse(sales$variance >= 0,
                          cols$variance_positive,
                          cols$variance_negative)
sales$label <- sprintf("%+.1f%%", sales$variance_pct)

ggplot(sales, aes(x = reorder(region, variance))) +
    # Reference bars (plan)
    geom_col(aes(y = plan), fill = NA, color = cols$actual,
             linewidth = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
    # Actual bars
    geom_col(aes(y = actual), fill = cols$actual, width = 0.4) +
    # Colored variance labels
    geom_marquee_colored(
        data = sales,
        mapping = aes(
            x = reorder(region, variance),
            y = actual + 8,
            label = label,
            color = var_color
        ),
        bold = TRUE,
        size = 3.5
    ) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(
        title = "Regional Sales Performance vs Plan",
        x = NULL, y = "Sales ($M)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()










Waterfall with Colored Value Labels

library(marquee)

#  Define colors for examples
cols <- list(actual = "#404040", actual_light = "#808080", variance_positive = "#006400", variance_negative = "#8B0000", previous = "#A0A0A0")

# Bridge data
bridge <- data.frame(
    driver = c("2023", "Volume", "Price", "Mix", "FX", "2024"),
    value = c(100, 15, 8, -5, -3, NA),
    type = c("start", "positive", "positive", "negative", "negative", "end")
)

# Calculate positions for waterfall
bridge$running <- cumsum(ifelse(is.na(bridge$value), 0, bridge$value))
bridge$running[nrow(bridge)] <- bridge$running[nrow(bridge) - 1]
bridge$ymin <- c(0, head(bridge$running, -1))
bridge$ymax <- bridge$running
bridge$ymin[bridge$type %in% c("start", "end")] <- 0

# Determine colors
bridge$bar_fill <- ifelse(
    bridge$type %in% c("start", "end"),
    cols$actual,
    cols$actual_light
)
bridge$label_color <- ifelse(
    bridge$type %in% c("start", "end"),
    "white",
    ifelse(bridge$value >= 0, cols$variance_positive, cols$variance_negative)
)

# Create labels
bridge$label <- ifelse(
    bridge$type %in% c("start", "end"),
    sprintf("%.0f", bridge$ymax),
    sprintf("%+.0f", bridge$value)
)

bridge$driver <- factor(bridge$driver, levels = bridge$driver)

ggplot(bridge, aes(x = driver)) +
    geom_rect(
        aes(xmin = as.numeric(driver) - 0.4,
            xmax = as.numeric(driver) + 0.4,
            ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax),
        fill = bridge$bar_fill,
        color = cols$actual
    ) +
    geom_marquee_colored(
        data = bridge,
        mapping = aes(
            x = driver,
            y = (ymin + ymax) / 2,
            label = label,
            color = label_color
        ),
        bold = TRUE,
        size = 4
    ) +
    labs(title = "Revenue Bridge with Colored Labels", x = NULL, y = "Revenue ($M)") +
    theme_minimal()

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
Marquee Syntax Reference
The marquee package uses a markdown-like syntax for formatting:



Syntax
Result




{.red text}
Red colored text


{#FF0000 text}
Hex-colored text


**text**
Bold text


*text*
Italic text


***text***
Bold italic text


{.red **text**}
Bold red text

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
Installation
If marquee is not installed, install it from CRAN:
install.packages("marquee")

title: Marquee Integration for Colored Labels
text:
Best Practices

Use sparingly - Colored text is powerful; don’t overuse it
Match semantics - Green for good, red for bad (in Western contexts)
Ensure readability - Make sure colors have sufficient contrast
Consider accessibility - Some users may have color vision deficiencies; use shape and position as additional indicators
Be consistent - Use the same color meanings throughout your report

title: stwd
text:
Source Code




MIT + file LICENSE



Citing this package




Alejandro Hagan (author, maintainer)

title: stwd
text:
Overview
Create presentation-ready visualizations following Storytelling with Data (SWD) principles. Provides story layouts that combine charts with narrative text, colored titles, and supporting context. Includes an interactive Shiny app for designing layouts, strategic color highlighting, and integration with the marquee package for colored text labels.

title: stwd
text:
Installation
# Install from Codeberg
remotes::install_git("https://codeberg.org/usrbinr/stwd")

title: stwd
text:
Key Features

Story Designer - Interactive Shiny app for designing layouts with real-time preview
Block Functions - Modular components (title, subtitle, narrative, caption) for patchwork composition
Highlight Colors - Strategic color use: gray out noise, highlight signal
Marquee Integration - Color-matched text in titles and labels
Color Palettes - 9 palette packages with manual color assignment

title: stwd
text:
Workflow
The stwd workflow is:

Create your plot with ggplot2
Design interactively with story_designer()
Copy the generated code from the Code tab
Paste into your Quarto document

title: stwd
text:
Quick Start

library(stwd)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

# 1. Create your base plot
df <- data.frame(
    product = c("Apples", "Oranges", "Bananas", "Grapes"),
    sales = c(120, 85, 95, 110)
)

fill_colors <- highlight_colors(
    df$product,
    highlight = c("Apples" = "#E63946")
)

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(product, sales), y = sales, fill = product)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Units Sold")

# 2. Launch the designer
story_designer(plot = p)

The designer generates code like this:

library(stwd)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

df <- data.frame(
    product = c("Apples", "Oranges", "Bananas", "Grapes"),
    sales = c(120, 85, 95, 110)
)

fill_colors <- highlight_colors(
    df$product,
    highlight = c("Apples" = "#E63946")
)

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(product, sales), y = sales, fill = product)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    theme_stwd() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Units Sold")

# Block components generated by story_designer()
title_plot <- title_block(
    "**Q4 Sales: {#E63946 Apples} Take the Lead**",
    title_size = 12
)

subtitle_plot <- subtitle_block(
    "Category performance, Oct-Dec 2024",
    subtitle_size = 10
)

narrative_plot <- text_narrative(
    "**Key takeaway:** {#E63946 Apples} outsold all other fruits.",
    size = 10
)

caption_plot <- caption_block(
    "Source: Internal Sales Database",
    caption_size = 8
)

# Combine with patchwork
content <- p + narrative_plot + plot_layout(widths = c(0.65, 0.35))
title_plot / subtitle_plot / content / caption_plot +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.82, 0.05))

title: stwd
text:
Story Designer
Launch the interactive designer to customize your layout:

story_designer(plot = p)




Story Designer


The app provides:

Text Controls - Title, subtitle, narrative, caption with marquee formatting
Legend Block - Inline colored text legend positioned above, below, left, or right of chart
Color Palettes - 9 palette packages: ggsci, MetBrewer, nord, PNWColors, rcartocolor, RColorBrewer, scico, viridis, wesanderson
Manual Colors - Set default color (gray) for unassigned categories, assign specific colors to highlight key categories
Validate & Export - Preview at actual size, export PNG/PDF/SVG, copy generated code

title: stwd
text:

Match your Quarto chunk options to the Story Designer’s export dimensions:

# Your patchwork code here...




Chunk Option
Story Designer

fig-width
Width (in)
Plot width in inches


fig-height
Height (in)
Plot height in inches


fig-dpi
DPI
Resolution (default 150)


out-width
-
Display width in document



See the Sizing Guide for more details.

title: Introduction to stwd
text: The stwd package provides tools for creating presentation-ready visualizations following Storytelling with Data principles. It helps you build complete “story layouts” that combine charts with narrative text, colored titles, and supporting context.

title: Introduction to stwd
text: The stwd package provides tools for creating presentation-ready visualizations following Storytelling with Data principles. It helps you build complete “story layouts” that combine charts with narrative text, colored titles, and supporting context.

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
Key Features
The package includes:

Story Designer - Interactive Shiny app for designing layouts with real-time preview
Block Functions - Modular components (title, subtitle, narrative, caption) for patchwork composition
Legend Block - Inline colored text legends embedded in the layout
Highlight Colors - Strategic color use (gray out noise, highlight signal)
Marquee Integration - Color-matched text in titles and labels

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
The SWD Approach
Traditional charts put the burden on the reader to interpret meaning. Storytelling with Data (SWD) charts guide the reader by:

Highlighting what matters - Gray out the noise, color the signal
Embedding the legend in the title - No separate legend box needed
Adding narrative context - Explain the “so what?” alongside the chart
Using a clear visual hierarchy - Big title, supporting chart, explanatory text

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
Workflow
The stwd workflow is:

Create your plot with ggplot2
Design interactively with story_designer()
Copy the generated code from the Code tab
Paste into your Quarto document

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
Quick Start

Step 1: Create Your Plot

# Sample data
df <- data.frame(
    product = c("Apples", "Oranges", "Bananas", "Grapes"),
    sales = c(120, 85, 95, 110)
)

# Highlight only the winner
fill_colors <- highlight_colors(
    df$product,
    highlight = c("Apples" = "#E63946")
)

# Create the base chart
p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(product, sales), y = sales, fill = product)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
    theme_stwd() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Units Sold")



Step 2: Launch the Designer
story_designer(plot = p)
The designer lets you:

Add and customize title, subtitle, narrative, caption
Adjust font sizes, alignment, spacing
Apply color palettes or manual colors
Preview at actual export size
Copy the generated code



Step 3: Use the Generated Code
The designer generates patchwork code like this:

# Block components
title_plot <- title_block(
    "**Q4 Sales: {#E63946 Apples} Take the Lead**",
    title_size = 12
)

subtitle_plot <- subtitle_block(
    "Fruit category performance, October-December 2024",
    subtitle_size = 10
)

narrative_plot <- text_narrative(
    "**Key takeaway:**

{#E63946 Apples} outsold all other fruits
this quarter, driven by seasonal demand
and promotional pricing.

**Recommendation:** Increase apple
inventory for Q1.",
    size = 10
)

caption_plot <- caption_block(
    "Source: Internal Sales Database",
    caption_size = 8
)

# Combine with patchwork
content <- p + narrative_plot + plot_layout(widths = c(0.65, 0.35))
title_plot / subtitle_plot / content / caption_plot +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.82, 0.05))

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
Story Designer Features
Text Controls:

Title, subtitle, narrative, caption with marquee formatting
Font size, alignment, line height, wrap width

Legend Block:

Inline colored text legend (e.g., “HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW”)
Position above, below, left, or right of chart

Color Palettes:

9 palette packages: ggsci, MetBrewer, nord, PNWColors, rcartocolor, RColorBrewer, scico, viridis, wesanderson
Click swatches to select specific colors
Apply to fill, color, or both

Manual Colors:

Set a default color (gray) for unassigned categories
Assign specific colors to highlight key categories
Perfect for the SWD approach: gray the noise, color the signal

Validate & Export:

Preview at actual export dimensions
PNG, PDF, or SVG export
Copy generated patchwork code

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
Inline Legends with legend_block()
Instead of a separate legend box, embed the legend in your layout:

legend_colors <- c("HIGH" = "#E63946", "MEDIUM" = "#808080", "LOW" = "#457B9D")

title_block("Performance by Category") /
  legend_block(legend_colors, halign = "right") /
  p /
  caption_block("SOURCE: Internal Data") +
  plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.04, 0.83, 0.05))

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
Learn More

Storytelling Layouts - Complete guide to story layouts
Sizing Guide - Matching Quarto dimensions to Story Designer
Marquee Integration - Colored text labels

title: Introduction to stwd
text:
References

Storytelling with Data
Recreating the SWD Look

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
When building complex story layouts with story_layout(), it can be difficult to understand how the various components fit together. The story_sitrep() function provides a “situation report” that shows you exactly what’s in your layout before you render it.
This is useful for:

Debugging - Understanding why elements overlap or look wrong
Planning - Previewing layout proportions before committing
Documentation - Seeing all parameters at a glance

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
When building complex story layouts with story_layout(), it can be difficult to understand how the various components fit together. The story_sitrep() function provides a “situation report” that shows you exactly what’s in your layout before you render it.
This is useful for:

Debugging - Understanding why elements overlap or look wrong
Planning - Previewing layout proportions before committing
Documentation - Seeing all parameters at a glance

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Basic Usage
story_sitrep() takes the same arguments as story_layout() and returns a diagnostic object:

sitrep <- story_sitrep(
    title = "My Report Title",
    subtitle = "Supporting context here",
    narrative = "Key insights and analysis",
    caption = "Source: Data Team"
)

sitrep




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• subtitle [text] (10pt)


• narrative [text] (10pt)


• caption [text] (6pt)


Not included: "plot"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "7%"


• Content height: "73%"


• Caption height: "5%"


• Narrative: "right" ("35%" width)


• Plot width: "65%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| SUBTITLE (7%)                                  |
|------------------------------------------------|
| NARRATIVE                                      |
| (73%)                                          |
|------------------------------------------------|
| CAPTION (5%)                                   |
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ Create visual hierarchy: title should be noticeably larger than subtitle


ℹ Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle


The output shows:

Components - Which elements are included, their type (text or ggplot), and size in points
Layout Proportions - How much vertical space each element takes
Layout Diagram - ASCII visualization of the structure
Tips - SWD-inspired suggestions for improvement

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Understanding the Layout Diagram
The ASCII diagram shows how your layout will be structured. When you have both a plot and narrative, the diagram shows their arrangement:

# Create a placeholder plot for demonstration
p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +
    geom_col() +
    theme_minimal()

# Layout with narrative on the RIGHT
story_sitrep(
    plot = p,
    title = "Sales Analysis",
    narrative = "Commentary here",
    narrative_position = "right"
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• plot [ggplot]


• narrative [text] (10pt)


Not included: "subtitle" and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "0%"


• Content height: "85%"


• Caption height: "0%"


• Narrative: "right" ("35%" width)


• Plot width: "65%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| PLOT                          |NARRATIVE       |
| 65%                           |35%             |
|                               |                |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ A subtitle can provide supporting context (time period, methodology, scope)


ℹ Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items


ℹ Including a source caption builds credibility with your audience


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle


Compare with narrative on the LEFT - notice how NARRATIVE and PLOT swap positions:

story_sitrep(
    plot = p,
    title = "Sales Analysis",
    narrative = "Commentary here",
    narrative_position = "left"
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• plot [ggplot]


• narrative [text] (10pt)


Not included: "subtitle" and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "0%"


• Content height: "85%"


• Caption height: "0%"


• Narrative: "left" ("35%" width)


• Plot width: "65%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| NARRATIVE      |PLOT                           |
| 35%            |65%                            |
|                |                               |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ A subtitle can provide supporting context (time period, methodology, scope)


ℹ Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items


ℹ Including a source caption builds credibility with your audience


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle


Or narrative at the BOTTOM (stacked vertically):

story_sitrep(
    plot = p,
    title = "Sales Analysis",
    narrative = "Commentary here",
    narrative_position = "bottom",
    narrative_width = 0.2
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• plot [ggplot]


• narrative [text] (10pt)


Not included: "subtitle" and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "0%"


• Content height: "85%"


• Caption height: "0%"


• Narrative: "bottom" ("20%" width)


• Plot width: "80%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| PLOT                                           |
|                                                |
|------------------------------------------------|
| NARRATIVE (20% height)                           |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ A subtitle can provide supporting context (time period, methodology, scope)


ℹ Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items


ℹ Including a source caption builds credibility with your audience


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Checking Proportions
The sitrep helps you verify that your height allocations make sense:

# Default proportions
story_sitrep(
    title = "Title",
    subtitle = "Subtitle",
    caption = "Caption"
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• subtitle [text] (10pt)


• caption [text] (6pt)


Not included: "plot" and "narrative"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "7%"


• Content height: "73%"


• Caption height: "5%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| SUBTITLE (7%)                                  |
|------------------------------------------------|
| CAPTION (5%)                                   |
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ Create visual hierarchy: title should be noticeably larger than subtitle


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle


If you have a long title that wraps to multiple lines, you may need more height:

# Increased title height for wrapping text
story_sitrep(
    title = "This is a Very Long Title That Will Wrap to Multiple Lines",
    subtitle = "Subtitle",
    title_height = 0.20
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• subtitle [text] (10pt)


Not included: "plot", "narrative", and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "20%"


• Subtitle height: "7%"


• Content height: "73%"


• Caption height: "0%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (20%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| SUBTITLE (7%)                                  |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ Create visual hierarchy: title should be noticeably larger than subtitle


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
SWD-Inspired Tips
The sitrep provides suggestions based on Storytelling with Data principles:

# Small title - sitrep suggests larger for visual hierarchy
story_sitrep(
    title = "Small Title",
    title_size = 10
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (10pt)


Not included: "subtitle", "plot", "narrative", and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "0%"


• Content height: "85%"


• Caption height: "0%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ A subtitle can provide supporting context (time period, methodology, scope)


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle



# Plot without narrative - sitrep suggests adding context
story_sitrep(
    plot = p,
    title = "Report"
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• plot [ggplot]


Not included: "subtitle", "narrative", and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "0%"


• Content height: "85%"


• Caption height: "0%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+

|------------------------------------------------|

|                                                |
| (85%)                                          |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ A subtitle can provide supporting context (time period, methodology, scope)


ℹ A narrative panel can explain the 'so what?' and guide your audience


ℹ Including a source caption builds credibility with your audience


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle



# Plot without source - sitrep suggests adding credibility
story_sitrep(
    plot = p,
    title = "Report",
    narrative = "Analysis"
)




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• plot [ggplot]


• narrative [text] (10pt)


Not included: "subtitle" and "caption"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "0%"


• Content height: "85%"


• Caption height: "0%"


• Narrative: "right" ("35%" width)


• Plot width: "65%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| PLOT                          |NARRATIVE       |
| 65%                           |35%             |
|                               |                |
|------------------------------------------------|
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ A subtitle can provide supporting context (time period, methodology, scope)


ℹ Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items


ℹ Including a source caption builds credibility with your audience


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Workflow: Sitrep Then Build
A recommended workflow is to check your sitrep before rendering:

# Step 1: Check the sitrep
my_sitrep <- story_sitrep(
    title = "**Q4 Results: {#E63946 Sales} Exceed Target**",
    subtitle = "Regional performance summary",
    narrative = "Key findings go here",
    narrative_position = "right",
    narrative_width = 0.35,
    caption = "Source: Finance"
)

my_sitrep




── Story Layout Sitrep ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────





── Components ──





• title [text] (12pt)


• subtitle [text] (10pt)


• narrative [text] (10pt)


• caption [text] (6pt)


Not included: "plot"





── Layout Proportions ──





• Title height: "15%"


• Subtitle height: "7%"


• Content height: "73%"


• Caption height: "5%"


• Narrative: "right" ("35%" width)


• Plot width: "65%"





── Layout Diagram ──





+------------------------------------------------+
| TITLE (15%)                                    |
|------------------------------------------------|
| SUBTITLE (7%)                                  |
|------------------------------------------------|
| NARRATIVE                                      |
| (73%)                                          |
|------------------------------------------------|
| CAPTION (5%)                                   |
+------------------------------------------------+


── Tips ──


ℹ Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message


ℹ Create visual hierarchy: title should be noticeably larger than subtitle


ℹ Colored text in title should match corresponding chart elements for consistency


ℹ Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items


ℹ Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle



# Step 2: If proportions look good, build the actual layout
df <- data.frame(
    region = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),
    sales = c(120, 95, 110, 85)
)

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(region, sales), y = sales)) +
    geom_col(fill = "#E63946") +
    coord_flip() +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Sales ($M)")

story_layout(
    plot = p,
    title = "**Q4 Results: {#E63946 Sales} Exceed Target**",
    subtitle = "Regional performance summary",
    narrative = "**North region** led all markets
with $120M in sales.

West underperformed at $85M,
requiring attention in Q1.",
    narrative_position = "right",
    narrative_width = 0.35,
    caption = "Source: Finance"
)

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Accessing Sitrep Data Programmatically
The sitrep is an S7 object. You can access its properties directly:

sitrep <- story_sitrep(
    title = "Report",
    subtitle = "Details",
    narrative = "Analysis"
)

# Access the components table
sitrep@components

  component present type size_pt
1     title    TRUE text      12
2  subtitle    TRUE text      10
3      plot   FALSE <NA>      NA
4 narrative    TRUE text      10
5   caption   FALSE <NA>      NA

# Access layout proportions
sitrep@layout$content_height

[1] 0.78

# Check for tips
sitrep@tips

[1] "Larger titles (14pt+) create visual hierarchy and draw attention to your message"
[2] "Create visual hierarchy: title should be noticeably larger than subtitle"        
[3] "Use **bold** in narrative to emphasize key takeaways and action items"           
[4] "Use auto_heights=TRUE in story_layout() to automatically size title/subtitle"

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Preventing Text Clipping with auto_heights
A common issue is titles or subtitles getting clipped when they wrap to multiple lines. The auto_heights parameter (default TRUE) automatically calculates appropriate heights:

# Long title that wraps - auto_heights handles it automatically
story_layout(
    plot = p,
    title = "**{#E63946 10 warehouses} need immediate attention for error rates**",
    subtitle = "What are the next steps at our highest volume sites?",
    caption = "Source: Operations Dashboard",
    auto_heights = TRUE,  # Default - calculates heights from text length
    output_width = 12     # Match your ggsave width for accurate estimates
)








For manual control, set auto_heights = FALSE and specify heights explicitly:

story_layout(
    plot = p,
    title = "Short title",
    auto_heights = FALSE,
    title_height = 0.10,
    subtitle_height = 0.06
)

Best practice: Keep titles under 60 characters. Concise, punchy titles are both easier to read AND less likely to have layout issues.

title: Debugging Layouts with story_sitrep()
text:
Summary



Function
Purpose




story_sitrep()
Preview layout structure before rendering


@components
Data frame of included elements


@layout
List of proportions and positions


@diagram
ASCII visualization


@tips
SWD-inspired suggestions



Use story_sitrep() to debug layouts, verify proportions, and get SWD-inspired suggestions before rendering.

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text: The stwd package includes functions inspired by Storytelling with Data principles for creating presentation-ready charts. These tools help you: - Combine charts with narrative text - Create self-documenting titles with colored category names - Use color strategically for emphasis - Build complete “story” layouts with one function call

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text: The stwd package includes functions inspired by Storytelling with Data principles for creating presentation-ready charts. These tools help you: - Combine charts with narrative text - Create self-documenting titles with colored category names - Use color strategically for emphasis - Build complete “story” layouts with one function call

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
The SWD Approach
Traditional charts put the burden on the reader to interpret meaning. SWD-style charts guide the reader by:

Highlighting what matters - Gray out the noise, color the signal
Embedding the legend in the title - No separate legend box needed
Adding narrative context - Explain the “so what?” alongside the chart
Using a clear visual hierarchy - Big title, supporting chart, explanatory text

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Quick Start
Use story_designer() to interactively design your layout, then copy the generated code. The designer produces patchwork code using block functions:

df <- data.frame(
    product = c("Apples", "Oranges", "Bananas", "Grapes"),
    sales = c(120, 85, 95, 110)
)

# Highlight only the winner
fill_colors <- highlight_colors(
    df$product,
    highlight = c("Apples" = "#E63946")
)

# Create the base chart
p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(product, sales), y = sales, fill = product)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
    theme_stwd() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Units Sold")

# Block components (generated by story_designer)
title_plot <- title_block(
    "**Q4 Sales: {#E63946 Apples} Take the Lead**",
    title_size = 12
)

subtitle_plot <- subtitle_block(
    "Fruit category performance, October-December 2024",
    subtitle_size = 10
)

narrative_plot <- text_narrative(
    "**Key takeaway:**

{#E63946 Apples} outsold all other fruits
this quarter, driven by seasonal demand
and promotional pricing.

Grapes came in second at 110 units,
while Oranges lagged at just 85.

**Recommendation:** Increase apple
inventory for Q1 to meet expected demand.",
    size = 10
)

caption_plot <- caption_block("Source: Internal Sales Database", caption_size = 8)

# Combine with patchwork
content <- p + narrative_plot + plot_layout(widths = c(0.65, 0.35))
title_plot / subtitle_plot / content / caption_plot +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.82, 0.05))

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Building Blocks

highlight_colors(): Strategic Color Use
Instead of coloring everything, highlight only what matters:

categories <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E")

# Single highlight with default blue
highlight_colors(categories, "C")

        A         B         C         D         E 
"#D3D3D3" "#D3D3D3" "#1E90FF" "#D3D3D3" "#D3D3D3" 



# Multiple highlights with custom colors
highlight_colors(
    categories,
    highlight = c("A" = "#E63946", "D" = "#2A9D8F")
)

        A         B         C         D         E 
"#E63946" "#D3D3D3" "#D3D3D3" "#2A9D8F" "#D3D3D3" 




marquee_title(): Self-Documenting Titles
Embed colored category names directly in your title:

fill_colors <- c(
    "Revenue" = "#2A9D8F",
    "Costs" = "#E63946",
    "Profit" = "#1E90FF"
)

marquee_title(
    "{Revenue} grew faster than {Costs}, boosting {Profit}",
    fill_colors
)

[1] "{#2A9D8F **Revenue**} grew faster than {#E63946 **Costs**}, boosting {#1E90FF **Profit**}"


Use with theme_marquee() to render the colors:

df <- data.frame(
    metric = c("Revenue", "Costs", "Profit"),
    value = c(150, 100, 50)
)

fill_colors <- c(
    "Revenue" = "#2A9D8F",
    "Costs" = "#E76F51",
    "Profit" = "#264653"
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = metric, y = value, fill = metric)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.6) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    labs(
        title = marquee_title(
            "{Revenue} minus {Costs} equals {Profit}",
            fill_colors
        ),
        x = NULL, y = "$ Millions"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme_marquee()










text_narrative(): Standalone Text Blocks
Create text panels for use with patchwork:

text_narrative(
    text = "**Key insight:**

This quarter showed {#E63946 strong growth}
in our core markets.

Action items:
- Expand capacity
- Hire additional staff",
    size = 3.5
)










Modular Block Functions
Each component of a story layout is available as a standalone function, giving you full control over sizing, positioning, and styling.

title_block(): Large Prominent Titles

title_block(
    "**Quarterly Performance Review**",
    title_size = 18
)










subtitle_block(): Supporting Context

subtitle_block(
    "{#2A9D8F Revenue} up 15% | {#E63946 Costs} down 3%",
    subtitle_size = 12
)










caption_block(): Source Attribution

caption_block("Source: Finance Department | Q4 2024")










legend_block(): Inline Colored Legends
Instead of a traditional legend box, embed colored category labels directly in your layout:

legend_colors <- c("HIGH" = "#E63946", "MEDIUM" = "#808080", "LOW" = "#457B9D")

legend_block(legend_colors, halign = "right", sep = " | ")








The legend can be positioned above, below, or beside the chart in your patchwork composition.


Combining Blocks Manually
For maximum control, combine blocks using patchwork:

df <- data.frame(x = c("A", "B", "C"), y = c(30, 50, 20))

chart <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y, fill = x)) +
    geom_col() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c(A = "#E63946", B = "#CCCCCC", C = "#CCCCCC"),
                      guide = "none") +
    theme_minimal()

# Stack components with custom heights
title_block("**{#E63946 Category A} Leads the Pack**", title_size = 18) /
subtitle_block("Year-over-year comparison by category") /
chart /
caption_block("Source: Internal Analytics") +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.83, 0.04))

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Manual Composition with Patchwork
For full control, build layouts manually:

# Data
df <- data.frame(
    quarter = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"),
    sales = c(85, 92, 88, 120)
)

# Colors - highlight Q4
fill_colors <- highlight_colors(
    df$quarter,
    highlight = c("Q4" = "#E63946")
)

# Main chart
chart <- ggplot(df, aes(x = quarter, y = sales, fill = quarter)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = "#666666") +
    annotate("text", x = 4.5, y = 102, label = "Target",
             hjust = 1, size = 3, color = "#666666") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Sales (units)")

# Narrative panel
narrative <- text_narrative(
    text = "**The {#E63946 Q4} breakthrough**

After three quarters of hovering
around 85-92 units, {#E63946 Q4}
finally broke through the 100-unit
target with **120 units sold**.

This 30% jump was driven by:

- Holiday promotions
- New product launch
- Expanded distribution

**Next steps:** Maintain momentum
into Q1 with continued marketing.",
    size = 3.2,
    valign = "top"
)

# Compose with patchwork
(chart + narrative) +
    plot_layout(widths = c(0.6, 0.4)) +
    plot_annotation(
        title = "**Annual Sales Trend: {#E63946 Q4} Exceeds Target**",
        subtitle = "Quarterly unit sales with 100-unit target line",
        caption = "Source: Sales Database | Analysis: Product Team",
        theme = theme(
            plot.title = element_marquee(size = 14),
            plot.subtitle = element_marquee(size = 10),
            plot.caption = element_marquee(size = 8)
        )
    )

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Colored Legend Text
Match legend text colors to bar fills using scale_fill_marquee():

df <- data.frame(
    category = c("Electronics", "Clothing", "Food", "Home"),
    sales = c(450, 320, 280, 190)
)

fill_colors <- c(
    "Electronics" = "#264653",
    "Clothing" = "#2A9D8F",
    "Food" = "#E9C46A",
    "Home" = "#E76F51"
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(category, sales), y = sales, fill = category)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_marquee(fill_colors, bold = TRUE) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.text = element_marquee()) +
    labs(
        title = "Sales by Category",
        x = NULL, y = "Revenue ($K)", fill = NULL
    )

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Adding Colored Labels to Bars
Use marquee_labels_from_colors() to create bar labels that match fills:

df <- data.frame(
    category = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),
    value = c(85, 120, 95, 110)
)

fill_colors <- c(
    "North" = "#457B9D",
    "South" = "#E63946",
    "East" = "#2A9D8F",
    "West" = "#F4A261"
)

# Add formatted labels
df$bar_label <- marquee_labels_from_colors(df$category, fill_colors, bold = TRUE)

ggplot(df, aes(x = category, y = value, fill = category)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    geom_marquee(aes(label = bar_label, y = 5), size = 4) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fill_colors, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(title = "Regional Performance", x = NULL, y = "Score")

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Complete Example: Executive Dashboard Panel
Putting it all together for a board-ready visual:

# Performance data
performance <- data.frame(
    region = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific", "Latin America"),
    actual = c(245, 180, 210, 85),
    target = c(220, 190, 200, 100)
)

performance$variance <- performance$actual - performance$target
performance$var_pct <- round(100 * performance$variance / performance$target, 1)

# Highlight regions that beat target
highlights <- setNames(
    ifelse(performance$variance >= 0, "#2A9D8F", "#CCCCCC"),
    performance$region
)
highlights["Asia Pacific"] <- "#1E90FF"  # Extra highlight for story

# Chart
chart <- ggplot(
    performance,
    aes(x = reorder(region, actual), y = actual, fill = region)
) +
    geom_col(width = 0.7) +
    geom_point(aes(y = target), shape = 4, size = 4, stroke = 1.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = highlights, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Revenue ($M)")

# Compose with block functions
title_plot <- title_block(
    "**Regional Revenue: {#2A9D8F North America} and {#1E90FF Asia Pacific} Exceed Targets**",
    title_size = 12
)

subtitle_plot <- subtitle_block(
    "FY2024 actual revenue vs. targets (X marks target)",
    subtitle_size = 10
)

narrative_plot <- text_narrative(
    "**Executive Summary**

Two of four regions exceeded their
annual revenue targets:

{#2A9D8F **North America**}: +$25M (+11%)
Driven by enterprise software deals

{#1E90FF **Asia Pacific**}: +$10M (+5%)
Strong growth in cloud services

**Underperformers:**

Europe missed by $10M due to
currency headwinds.

Latin America fell short by $15M;
restructuring plan underway.

**FY2025 Outlook:** Cautiously
optimistic with 8% growth target.",
    size = 10
)

caption_plot <- caption_block(
    "Source: Finance | Prepared for Q4 Board Meeting",
    caption_size = 8
)

content <- chart + narrative_plot + plot_layout(widths = c(0.62, 0.38))
title_plot / subtitle_plot / content / caption_plot +
    plot_layout(heights = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.82, 0.05))

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Tips for Effective Storytelling Charts

Lead with the insight - Your title should state the conclusion, not just describe the data
Use color sparingly - Gray is your friend; color only what matters
Integrate the legend - Embed category colors in titles or narrative text
Provide context - The narrative panel explains “so what?” and “now what?”
Design for your audience - Executives want conclusions; analysts want details
Keep text readable - Don’t shrink text to fit more in; simplify instead

title: Storytelling with Data Layouts
text:
Function Reference



Function
Purpose




story_designer()
Interactive Shiny app for layout design


title_block()
Create title plots


subtitle_block()
Create subtitle plots


text_narrative()
Create narrative text blocks


caption_block()
Create caption plots


legend_block()
Create inline colored text legend


highlight_colors()
Gray out non-essential categories


theme_stwd()
Clean SWD theme (horizontal grid only)


theme_marquee()
Enable marquee for all text elements


scale_fill_marquee()
Match legend text to fill colors


marquee_title()
Embed colored names in titles


marquee_labels_from_colors()
Create colored bar labels
